title: By the Numbers: What Indians Actually Do Online
thesis: India's digital-access success has produced a usage mix dominated by entertainment and social media, with education and production occupying a far smaller share of online time than the country's demographic dividend requires.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: India's digital-access success has produced a usage mix dominated by entertainment and social media, with education and production occupying a far smaller share of online time than the country's demographic dividend requires.

evidence.snippet: NCAER IHDS Wave 3 finds that roughly two-thirds of reported internet use is for entertainment while about one-sixth is for education.
The Internet in India Report 2024 documents near-ubiquitous access and a usage mix dominated by entertainment, social media, and messaging.

counterevidence.summary: The same platforms are also used for peer learning, exam groups, and informal skill discovery, so the boundary between social and educational use is fuzzy.

claims.claim: The NCAER India Human Development Survey Wave 3 found that roughly two-thirds of reported internet use is for entertainment while only about one-sixth is for education, a ratio of roughly 4:1.

evidence.snippet: 66% of internet usage is for entertainment; 16.1% is for education or online courses/classes.

counterevidence.summary: Reported shares reflect respondent self-classification and may not map cleanly onto actual minutes; multi-purpose use can be classified differently by different users.

claims.claim: ASER 2024 reports that 76% of 14–16-year-olds use smartphones for social media while 57% use them for education.

evidence.snippet: Among 14–16-year-olds, 76% use smartphones for social media while 57% use them for education.

counterevidence.summary: The same device can be used for both social and educational purposes, and the survey does not measure time spent or learning outcomes.

claims.claim: Indian adolescents and young adults are estimated to spend several hours per day on screens, with a large majority exceeding the two-hour daily screen-time recommendation.

evidence.snippet: A review of Indian adolescent screen-time studies reports averages of 4–5 hours per day across devices and activities.
A rural Indian study found that 83.2% of secondary school children spent more than the recommended two hours per day on screen devices.

counterevidence.summary: Screen-time figures vary widely by age, region, income, and measurement method; no single nationally representative census exists.

claims.claim: The NSSO Time Use Survey 2024 shows that 93% of Indians aged 6 and above participate in mass media or leisure activities, and leisure time has risen about 15% since 2019.

evidence.snippet: 93% of Indians aged 6 and above participate in mass media or leisure activities; leisure time has risen about 15% since 2019.

counterevidence.summary: The survey captures activity participation, not platform-specific attention or quality of leisure.

claims.claim: Gallup data places India's employee engagement at 23% and estimates roughly $351 billion in annual losses from disengagement, illustrating the economic opportunity cost of attention fragmentation.

evidence.snippet: Gallup State of the Global Workplace 2025 places India's employee engagement at 23% and estimates $351 billion in annual losses from disengagement.

counterevidence.summary: Engagement is shaped by wages, job quality, management, and macroeconomic conditions, not only by digital distraction.

claims.claim: Entertainment is not the problem; the problem is the ratio of entertainment to education, production, and rest, and the opportunity cost that ratio imposes.

evidence.snippet: The large entertainment-to-education usage ratio implies an opportunity cost for learning and skill-building time.
India's digital economy contributes 11.74% of GDP, while Gallup estimates large disengagement losses, showing that the same infrastructure creates value and opportunity cost simultaneously.

counterevidence.summary: Leisure and entertainment have legitimate social and psychological roles; the concern is proportion, not elimination.

sources.title: NCAER: The Evolving Landscape of Digital Inclusion in India (IHDS Wave 3)

agentInstructions: Use claim IDs as the retrieval unit and treat each claim as separate from solution chapters.
Preserve the plain-language, evidence-cautious tone; do not sensationalize screen-time findings.
When citing sources, use the source IDs and URLs from the artifact; do not invent or generalize beyond the public evidence.
Flag any India figure that is a global benchmark or industry estimate so readers do not mistake it for a national measurement.
